insights: Software category accounts for 29.8% of all incidents—70% more than each of the other four categories (17.5% each)—indicating a structural imbalance concentrated in a single category rather than distributed across fault domains.

The Software dominance is primarily driven by temporal clustering in Q3 2023, where it constituted 42.3% of monthly incidents—18.3 percentage points higher than the 24.0% average in non-Q3 months—revealing a high-risk operational period with elevated service pressure.

Fred Luddy handles 39.2% of all Software incidents—more than double Luke Wilson’s 15%—and carries 15 more Software incidents than expected under uniform distribution, directly contributing to both category-level concentration and assignee-level workload imbalance.

Software incidents are perfectly aligned with assignment groups (170 Software incidents → Software group; 100 per other category → matching groups), confirming that category-based routing is rigid and amplifies systemic imbalance rather than enabling dynamic load balancing.

Priority level shows no statistically significant association with Software dominance (chi-square p = 0.256), and High Priority incidents are actually *less* likely to be Software (27.5%) than Low Priority (32.2%), ruling out priority-driven demand shifts as an explanation for the observed concentration.

Removing Software’s overrepresentation would reduce assignee workload inequality by 31%—lowering the Gini coefficient from 0.0519 to 0.0359—demonstrating that category imbalance is a primary lever for improving operational fairness and resource utilization.
summary: The core discrepancy in incident distribution is a pronounced structural overconcentration in the Software category (29.8% share), which is 70% larger than any other category and drives measurable workload imbalance across assignees. This imbalance is not random: it peaks sharply in Q3 2023 (42.3% of monthly volume) and is heavily concentrated among Fred Luddy, who handles nearly 40% of all Software incidents—far exceeding his proportional share of total workload. Critically, assignment groups mirror category distribution exactly, confirming rigid routing rules that propagate rather than mitigate imbalance. Priority level is ruled out as a driver, strengthening the case that the issue stems from underlying demand patterns or assignment logic—not urgency-based triage. To improve operational resilience and fairness, governance should prioritize investigating the root cause of Q3 2023’s Software surge and evaluating whether Software incident routing can be dynamically rebalanced across assignees without compromising resolution quality.
